retrieved_docs.content: Most cystic masses arise at the infrahyoid level (65% of the cases) and result from the secretion of the epithelial lining that remained from the non-obliterated duct, resulting in possible inflammation and, therefore, the formation of a thyroglossal duct cyst. These are the most common cause of midline neck masses around the world.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyrohyoid Membrane -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngoceles are anomalous air sacs within the supraglottic larynx and are classified as internal or external based on their relationship to the thyrohyoid membrane. Internal laryngoceles are comprised of a collection of air or serous fluid in the anterior portion of the laryngeal ventricle within the confines of the thyroid cartilage, versus external laryngoceles whose sacs protrude through the thyrohyoid membrane. Internal laryngoceles will typically present as cough, hoarseness, odynophagia, or airway obstruction. External laryngoceles will cause an anterior neck mass resembling an abscess or cyst. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyrohyoid Membrane -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Transection of the thyrohyoid membrane is rare but can be fatal. A prompt diagnosis and securing airway patency are essential in any patient with a neck injury. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Abscess -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Particular red flags include neck asymmetry, neck masses, lymphadenopathy, trismus, medial displacement of the lateral pharyngeal wall and tonsil, torticollis, cranial nerve involvement, spiking fevers, and tachypnea/shortness of breath.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid Thyroglossal Duct -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure consists of a neck incision in which we go up the hyoid bone located in the upper neck. The mid-portion of the hyoid bone is then removed along with a small amount of tongue tissue. This last step, removal of the mid-portion of the hyoid bone along with a slight bit of tongue tissue, is done to prevent the reoccurrence of a cystic mass.

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroid Nodule -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory neck masses usually represent enlarged lymph nodes which may be viral or bacterial in etiology. These are commonly located superficial, deep, or posterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle, and anterior to the trapezius muscle. [23] Some non-thyroid neoplastic disorders may present as metastatic neck masses; these are most commonly associated with squamous cell carcinoma from the aerodigestive tract. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyroid -- Clinical Significance -- Goiter
retrieved_docs.content: Goiter refers to thyroid gland enlargement, ranging from mild asymmetry to massive growth that causes airway compression (see Image . Goiter in a Woman). Common etiologies include iodine deficiency, hyperthyroidism, and hypothyroidism. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: 3-3-2 Rule -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: 3: A measurement of 3 fingers from the anterior tip of the mandible to the anterior neck estimates the volume of the submandibular space. In a typical patient, it is possible to place 3 fingers on the floor of the mandible between the mental angle and the neck in the vicinity of the hyoid bone. Under normal circumstances, this distance should measure approximately 7 cm. If the measurement is less than 3 finger widths, it suggests that the laryngeal axis is more acute than the pharyngeal axis. This indicates that aligning the oral opening to the pharyngeal opening will be challenging. Furthermore, it suggests that there will be limited space available for the displacement of the tongue within the throat. The 3-finger rule has certain limitations, as the distance can vary based on factors such as height and ethnicity. To address these limitations, an alternative method known as the ratio of height-to-thyromental distance (RHTMD) has been proposed.

Those experiencing postoperative bleeding, neurological disorders, or alcohol use disorder are more likely to return to the emergency room or be readmitted within 48 hours after surgery. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Thyroglossal Duct Cyst -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroglossal duct cysts typically present as mobile masses in the midline of the neck, positioned near the hyoid bone. Although they are often asymptomatic, they can occasionally manifest as an abscess or a sinus that drains intermittently. The thyroglossal mass moves upward when the tongue is protruded or during swallowing. Additionally, thyroglossal duct cysts are closely associated with the hyoid bone and are most commonly located at or below the level of the hyoid.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroglossal duct ordinarily disappears, but remnants of its epithelium may remain, allowing for the potential development of a thyroglossal duct cyst in this area. These cysts generally occur in the neck, close to or inferior to the body of the hyoid bone. They form a swelling at the anterior midline of the neck that is painless, fluctuant, and moves upon swallowing. Thyroglossal duct cysts are typically removed surgically. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 872
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck from primary malignant neoplasm of thyroid, primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone, and primary malignant neoplasm of thymus are all examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, which are pathological processes. Each represents a malignant tumor arising in a specific anatomic site—thyroid cancer that has spread to cervical lymph nodes, a malignant growth originating in the nasal bone, and a malignant tumor arising in the thymus. Recognizing these entities as primary malignant neoplastic proliferations highlights their aggressive, disease‑causing nature and underscores the need for thorough oncologic assessment and appropriate therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2551
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of thyroid is categorized as an اضطراب صماوي (endocrine disorder) and is anatomically associated with head and neck structures. This connection highlights that thyroid malignancies arise within the endocrine system and involve the anatomical region of the head and neck, which is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of neck is classified as a specific form of نقائل إلى الرقبة (neck metastases), indicating that this particular cancer spread to the cervical lymph nodes falls under the broader category of metastases to the neck. This relationship underscores the importance of recognizing cervical lymph‑node involvement as a distinct manifestation of neck metastasis in oncologic evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1924

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2002
retrieved_docs.content: Chest imaging for metastatic carcinoma of thyroid is a diagnostic procedure that directly targets the chest (hrudník) and incorporates a radioisotope study of the endocrine system as a subordinate component. Closed drainage of chest is a therapeutic intervention performed within the chest region, which serves as an indirect procedural site and is associated with the subdivision of the thorax. Both the imaging study and the drainage procedure involve the chest area, linking diagnostic evaluation of metastatic thyroid cancer to therapeutic management of thoracic conditions such as pleural effusion, and highlighting the relevance of coordinated imaging and interventional techniques in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Head and Neck Surgery is a medical specialty that includes procedures such as بضع طبلة الأذن, which is classified as a type of Head and Neck Surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1146
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of the thyroid is classified as a subtype of اضطراب صماوي (thyroid disorder) and is clinically associated with استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). The broader procedure category thyroidectomy; partial or complete serves as a parent concept for both استئصال الدرقية (total thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). Partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with دراق (the thyroid gland) itself, reflecting the close anatomical and therapeutic relationship between the gland and its surgical removal. This network of relationships underscores that thyroid cancer is viewed within the spectrum of thyroid disorders and that its management commonly involves either total or partial thyroidectomy, each linked to the underlying thyroid tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically linked to the surgical treatment known as thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a procedure that specifically targets the head and neck structure. The term thyroidectomy; partial or complete is a detailed form of the broader Arabic term استئصال الدرقية, which serves as its parent designation. This relationship underscores that thyroidectomy (partial or complete) is the primary operative intervention for managing cancer of the thyroid within the head and neck region.
